skill.name: requesting-code-review
skill.description: Guides agents to request structured code review after development tasks and before merging changes.
skill.summary: Request structured code review for completed changes before moving forward.
skill.author: Cycleaddict

security_audit.summary: Static findings were mostly false positives from git SHA wording, example review text, and command snippets inside documentation. The confirmed risk is that the skill instructs agents to run external git commands, including unquoted revision placeholders in the review template.

medium_findings.title: External Git Commands with Placeholder Arguments
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run git diff, git rev-parse, git log, grep, head, and awk commands. The review template uses unquoted {BASE_SHA} and {HEAD_SHA} placeholders, so unsafe placeholder values could alter the shell command if not validated by the agent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command snippets are explicit and intended to be executed by an agent. The commands are legitimate git review operations, but placeholder validation is not specified.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Git SHA References
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography findings match the term SHA in git revision labels and placeholders. No hashing code, weak digest algorithm, credential handling, or cryptographic decision was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain descriptive text, placeholders, or code review checklist wording. They do not implement cryptography.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Review Checklist Text
low_findings.description: Static system-reconnaissance findings match ordinary review guidance and example file references. No commands collect host, network, process, account, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are part of review instructions or sample output. They do not request operating system reconnaissance or secret discovery.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unquoted Shell Placeholders in Git Diff Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The template places {BASE_SHA} and {HEAD_SHA} directly inside shell commands. Agents should only execute these commands after confirming both values are valid git revisions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are real examples with placeholder interpolation. The risk depends on how callers fill the placeholders.

content.value_statement: Teams often miss defects when they move from implementation to merge without a structured review step. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable process for requesting focused code review.
actual_capabilities: Prompts agents to request review after completed tasks, major features, and pre-merge work.
Provides git range fields for comparing a base commit and head commit.
Defines a review checklist for quality, architecture, tests, requirements, and production readiness.
Specifies severity categories for critical, important, and minor issues.
Guides agents to act on review feedback before continuing development.
limitations: Requires valid git revisions and repository access to review diffs.
Does not run tests or verify builds by itself.
Depends on the reviewing agent to inspect the actual changed files.
Does not include automatic protection against unsafe shell placeholder values.

use_cases.title: Review completed feature work
use_cases.description: Ask for a structured review after a feature is implemented and before the next task starts.

use_cases.title: Check merge readiness
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to decide whether code is ready to merge or needs fixes first.

use_cases.title: Improve agent-driven development
use_cases.description: Add review checkpoints between batches of AI-assisted implementation work.

prompt_templates.title: Review my latest change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the requesting-code-review skill to review my latest completed change. Compare the previous commit to HEAD and report critical, important, and minor issues.

prompt_templates.title: Review against a plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the requesting-code-review skill to review this implementation against the linked plan. Check requirements, tests, architecture, and production readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare for merge
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the requesting-code-review skill before merging. Review the git range from the base branch to HEAD and give a clear merge readiness verdict.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a multi-task implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the requesting-code-review skill to review the full task batch. Identify regressions, missed requirements, test gaps, and any fixes required before continuing.

output_examples.input: Review a new verification function against the implementation plan.
output: Strengths: Clear structure, focused tests, and readable error handling.
Important issue: Missing progress reporting for long operations.
Assessment: Ready with fixes before the next task.

output_examples.input: Check whether the current branch is ready to merge.
output: Important issues: Add validation for invalid date input.
Assessment: Merge after the validation fix and one focused test.

best_practices: Use exact base and head revisions from git before requesting review.
Include the plan or requirements so the reviewer can compare intent with implementation.
Fix critical and important findings before continuing to the next task.
anti_patterns: Skipping review because the change looks small.
Providing vague requirements that prevent meaningful comparison.
Executing git commands with untrusted revision strings.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews code changes, requirements alignment, tests, architecture, and production readiness.

faq.question: Does it replace human code review?
faq.answer: No. It adds an AI review checkpoint, but human ownership remains important for production code.

faq.question: Does it run tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It asks the reviewer to check test quality and status, but it does not execute tests by itself.

faq.question: Why does it need git commands?
faq.answer: It uses git revisions to identify the exact code range that should be reviewed.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it instructs agents to run external git commands.

faq.question: How should users reduce risk?
faq.answer: Use validated git commit hashes or branch names, and avoid passing untrusted text into shell commands.
